EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A seasonally strong low pressure system lifted northeast from northern Kansas to extreme western Iowa, on March 31, 2007. A warm front moved northward into northeast Iowa followed by a cold front sweeping across eastern Iowa into Illinois. These frontal boundaries triggered thunderstorms, and with sufficient low level wind shear, allowed for many to rotate. Most of the thunderstorms produced large hail and damaging wind.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sheriffs department reported many trees down, some blocking roads in the Woodbine area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong area of low pressure lifted from the central Plains into the western Great Lakes during the day. Thunderstorms fired along a frontal boundary that extended southeast from this system through central Illinois.  These storms produced several reports of severe wind gusts and hail.  A brief tornado touched down near the town of Elwin, causing one injury.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The tornado touched down 1/2 mile southwest of the intersection of Elwin Road and Rt. 51 and ended 1/2 mile to the north northeast.  The tornado caused damage to a house porch and church chimney as well as numerous trees.  A two foot diameter tree fell on three cars causing severe damage.  One minor injury occurred to an 18 year old female when she was blown to the ground.  Maximum wind speed with the tornado was estimated at 80 MPH.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An extended period of cold weather occurred across central and southeast Illinois during the first two weeks of April.  Several hard freezes occurred at night during this time.  The cold snap occurred after a period of unseasonably mild weather in late March which resulted in plants and flowers leafing out and blooming earlier than normal.  The hard freeze caused considerable damage to the plants that started their growth early due to the warm conditions in late March. The most significant agricultural damage occurred to winter wheat, mainly in locations along and south of I-72. Damage estimates will be unknown until late summer.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: March roared in like a lion, as an intense low pressure system tracked from Oklahoma to central Iowa, and then into the western Great Lakes. Strong winds accompanied this storm across the region, with snow and blowing snow a significant problem in eastern Iowa and northwest Illinois. Snowfall amounts ranged from 1 to 3 inches mainly north of Highway 34. Sustained winds of 35 to 40 mph with gusts of 45 to 50 mph were common across the area. Isolated gusts over 50 mph were measured. This created dangerous whiteout conditions. Besides the snow, there was locallized heavy rain of .75 to 1.5 inches falling on partially frozen ground, which caused significant ponding of water.

A seasonally strong low pressure system lifted northeast from northern Kansas to extreme western Iowa, on March 31, 2007. A warm front moved northward into northeast Iowa followed by a cold front sweeping across eastern Iowa into Illinois. These frontal boundaries triggered thunderstorms, and with sufficient low level wind shear, allowed for many to rotate. Most of the thunderstorms produced large hail and damaging wind.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sheriffs department reported several power lines down along with some smaller branches in the Hennepin area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An extended period of cold weather occurred across central and southeast Illinois during the first two weeks of April.  Several hard freezes occurred at night during this time.  The cold snap occurred after a period of unseasonably mild weather in late March which resulted in plants and flowers leafing out and blooming earlier than normal.  The hard freeze caused considerable damage to the plants that started their growth early due to the warm conditions in late March. The most significant agricultural damage occurred to winter wheat, mainly in locations along and south of I-72. Damage estimates will be unknown until late summer.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of thunderstorms along a strong cold front reached southeast Missouri by mid-afternoon. As this line of storms approached the Mississippi River, new thunderstorm development occurred over western Kentucky and southern Illinois. These new storms formed in a very warm and unstable atmosphere heated by almost unabated sunshine. The new storms turned out to produce most of the severe weather reports, consisting mostly of marginally severe hail and wind gusts to around 60 MPH. The initial line of storms over southeast Missouri weakened as it moved east, crossing into cooler air that was stabilized by the new thunderstorms. Winds in the mid and upper levels of the atmosphere were marginally favorable for organized storm structures. Some of the storms evolved into small lines capable of damaging winds. The wind shear, or change of wind speed and direction with height, was not supportive of storm rotation. As a result, all of the reported wind damage was the result of straight-line winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees were blown down across roads, including Highway 37.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several rounds of heavy rain-producing showers and thunderstorms moved across eastern Iowa and west central Illinois from 12:00 am June 22nd until approximately 6:00 am June 23rd.  This occurred on already saturated ground from heavy rains on June 21st.  The areas from Waterloo, to Cedar Rapids, to Iowa City, to Wapello, to Burlington, to Macomb were affected the most receiving rainfall totals of 4 to 8 inches.  Flash flooding was observed across much of the affected area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Water 8-inches deep covered 900E just south of 900N.

A line of thunderstorms pivoted northeast into parts of southeast Iowa during the mid-morning hours of June 1st. Much of the area was just breaking out of a blanket of dense fog where visibilities dropped to less than a quarter mile. Dew point temperatures were in the middle 60s to around 70 degrees.||The line of storms appeared to become more broken through the late morning hours, while the area from Iowa City to Waterloo appeared to stratify out into a large area of showers. Just before 12:00 pm CDT, rapid intensification of storm cells on the southeast end of the original line occurred as it moved into northern portions of Louisa County.||A tornado touched down just south of Grandview, IA and moved northeast through Fruitland, IA and on to the southwest parts of Muscatine, IA. The tornado then lifted and as the storm cell continued to move northeast across Muscatine County. The super-cell re-intensified as it entered the southeast part of Cedar County just before 1 pm producing a brief tornado near Wilton, IA. The storm then moved across northwest parts of Scott County and Clinton County producing damaging wind gusts and large hail. The super-cell continued northeast into Jackson County producing a tornado near Bellevue around 2:30 pm, which moved across the Mississippi River into Jo Daviess County before lifting. The storm produced yet another tornado just south of Scales Mound, IL around 3:15 pm before moving into southwest Wisconsin and dissipating.||During the early afternoon hours, additional storms strengthened on the south end of the original line of storms, which went on to produce wind damage and large hail as they moved through northwest Illinois through the late afternoon hours.
A line of severe thunderstorms brought hail and some tree damage to East Central Missouri and Southwest Illinois.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm spotters reported 3/4 inch hail south of Fieldon.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong storms producing severe weather and flooding during the afternoon of June 27th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nickel size hail reported at Interstate 355 and Finley Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of thunderstorms pivoted northeast into parts of southeast Iowa during the mid-morning hours of June 1st. Dew point temperatures were in the middle 60s to around 70 degrees.||The line of storms appeared to become more broken through the late morning hours, while the area from Iowa City to Waterloo appeared to stratify out into a large area of showers. The storm then moved across northwest parts of Scott County and Clinton County producing damaging wind gusts and large hail. The super-cell continued northeast into Jackson County producing a tornado near Bellevue around 2:30 pm, which moved across the Mississippi River into Jo Daviess County before lifting. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms developed during the afternoon hours of June 18th and moved across northerast Illinois, producing mainly wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An area of Morris suffered tree damage and the falling trees and tree limbs damaged several houses.  Some trees were 2 to 3 feet in diameter.  The area of damage was bounded by Vine Street to the west, the railroad tracks to the north, Jeffereson Street to the south and Washington Street on the east.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong storms produced heavy rain which caused flooding across parts of northern Illinois, as well as some localized wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One foot of water was reported near Granville and McCormick.  One foot of standing water at Lincoln and Devon.  One foot of water in the viaduct at Oak Street and Lake Shore Drive with two cars stuck in the flood waters.  Exit ramps flooded and closed at Irving Park and Belmont exiting the Kennedy Expressway.  Every exit viaduct on Lake Shore Drive flooded and impassable from Randolph north to Hollywood.  Car stuck in flood waters with a person still in the car at LaSalle Drive exit from Lake Shore Drive.  A taxi was submerged in flood waters at Irving Park Road and Lake Shore Drive.  The rain caused a roof of a residental building to cave in on Elston.  Hundreds of basements were flooded.  The dugouts were also flooded at Wrigley Field.  A rain gage two miles northwest of Lincoln Park in Chicago measured 3.09 inches of rain between 1245 pm CST and 245 pm CST.  A rain gage in Jefferson Park measured 2.30 inches of rain in one hour between 155 pm CST and 255 pm CST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms moved across parts of north central Illinois producing wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts estimated to 60 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe drought conditions developed across much of southeast Illinois during September 2007.  While precipitation levels were below normal for much of the year, the combination of extended precipitation deficits and unseasonably hot conditions in August and much of September started to impact crop health.  The severe drought conditions extended into October 2007.  The drought conditions eased by late October when 2 to 4 inches of rainfall fell across the drought region.

Severe storms moved across northwest Illinois during the early morning hours of August 14th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large parts of two trees were blown down, both onto power lines.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A decaying line of thunderstorms produced severe hail near Galesburg.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Estimated penny size hail at Pinkys Cozy Inn on Route 150.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe storms moved across northern Illinois during the early morning hours August 22nd.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A spotter measured a wind gust to 63 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An isolated thunderstorm produced an area of wind damage during the evening hours on the 22nd.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous trees were blown down, especially in the city of Pekin.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large severe thunderstorm bow echo moved across portions of Knox, Stark, Marshall, and Peoria counties during the afternoon hours of the 23rd causing significant damage.||Widespread damage occurred across the city of Galesburg by wind gusts in the 70 to 80 mph range.  These wind speeds continued for several miles past Galesburg, to the Wataga and Victoria areas.  Within the city of Galesburg, enhanced damage was caused by a fast-moving microburst, which produced estimated wind speeds of around 100 mph.||Within the city of Galesburg, the area of damage was about 8 miles wide.  A wind gust of 74 mph was reported at the Galesburg Municipal Airport at 1:09 pm, before the automated weather station failed.  Damage began in the city of Galesburg around 1:10 pm, and continued within the city limits for approximately 5 minutes.  There were more than 500 trees either damaged or destroyed.  Falling trees or large tree limbs resulted in significant damage to dozens of buildings and vehicles.  Numerous buildings sustained damage to roofing and chimneys, as well as minor damage to siding and broken windows.  About 20 power poles were snapped, and numerous power lines were brought down.  At least two semi-trailers were blown over on U.S. Highway 34 west of Galesburg, and on I-74 just east of Galesburg.||An area of enhanced wind damage was found within the city as well.  This area was about 1/2 mile wide, and 2 miles long.  It began close to the intersection of Knox and Pine Streets, and continued to the northeast, crossing the intersections of Grand Avenue and Oak Street, Farnham and East Main Street, and extending to Illini Drive and the Soangetaha Country Club east of I-74.  Significant damage to trees, fences, garages and a business occurred around 1:15 pm.  Several homes sustained roof and siding damage, primarily from debris and falling tree limbs.||The storm injured 7 people in Galesburg, with cuts and broken bones||The city of Knoxville, about 4 miles southeast of Galesburg, also sustained widespread wind damage, shortly after 1:15 pm.  Wind speeds were estimated to be 60-80 mph.  Extensive damage was done to trees and power lines.  Roofs and autos were damaged, mainly from falling trees and limbs.||Wataga, 4 miles northeast of Galesburg, was affected by the high winds around 1:20 pm.  Winds estimated at 60-80 mph also produced damage to trees, and downed power lines.||The town of Victoria, 15 miles east-northeast of Galesburg, also sustained 60-80 mph wind gusts.  The damaging wind struck around 1:25 pm, downing trees and power lines.  A man was killed in rural Victoria after a falling tree struck him.||Numerous farm fields sustained damage in northern Knox County, due to the high winds.  Corn stalks and soybean plants were flattened as a result.||Additional tree, crop and power line damage occurred across Stark, Marshall and northern Peoria counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A swath of thunderstorm wind damage cut across Stark county.  The winds blew down numerous trees, power lines, and corn fields.  A church in Toulon also had some windows blown out.|

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain-producing showers and thunderstorms moved across eastern Iowa and northwest Illinois during the evening and overnight hours of July 18th.  An isolated storm downed some trees in Warren, IL around 7 PM.  Later, a bowing segment of thunderstorms pushed into Carroll County resulting in more wide-spread wind damage.  Rainfall totals ranged between three quarters of an inch to just under 3 inches, but due to saturated soils some flooding problems developed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flood waters covered portions of Mill St near the city garage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms moved south across northeast Illinois during the early morning hours of July 26th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Roof damage to two large commercial buildings.  Numerous large limbs blown down.  Damage concentrated near County Line Road and Interstate 55.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An isolated thunderstorm produced wind damage near Buzzville.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A few large trees and numerous tree limbs were blown down.|

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A bowing thunderstorm complex dove southeast from northeast Iowa to central Illinois during the morning hours of July 17th producing damaging winds in Kewanee, IL around 7 AM.  Torrential rains also accompanied the storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds estimated to be 70 mph downed a tree 1.5 to 2 feet in diameter.  Several limbs 6 to 8 inches in diameter were also blown down as well as some power and phone lines.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds estimated to be 65 mph blew down several trees onto Cory Rd.  Some fuse boxes were damaged.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flood waters covered Winslow Rd just southeast of Warren, IL.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Additional thunderstorms moved across Stark and Marshall counties during the evening hours.  The damage associated with these storms was not as widespread as those earlier in the day.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees and numerous corn stalks were blown down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong to severe thunderstorms developed along and ahead of an outflow boundary.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A swath of damaging winds went through the town of Hord.  26 homes received minor damage. 20 outbuildings were damaged and 6 were destroyed.  2 businesses had minor damage.  10 utility poles were destroyed.  Numerous trees and tree limbs were blown down and several corn fields were flattened.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong to severe thunderstorms developed along and ahead of an outflow boundary.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees, power poles and power lines were blown down.|

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong to severe thunderstorms developed along and ahead of an outflow boundary.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous trees, tree limbs, and power lines were blown down.  A man was injured by a falling tree limb in Wheeler.  Shingles and tin were blown off a roof in Newton.  A semi trailer was blown over between Wheeler and Newton.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level trough and surface cold front approached the region, touching off showers and thunderstorms. Many of these thunderstorms moved slowly or almost not at all. This produced isolated minor flooding problems.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall produced some minor flooding of secondary streets in Mount Carmel. Several vehicles including a jeep became stranded in high water on city streets. The grounds of a middle school were partially flooded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe storms moved across northern Illinois during the late evening hours of August 22nd.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees blown down onto power lines.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe storms moved across northern Illinois during the late evening hours of August 22nd.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Northbound Michigan Avenue entrance tunnel to northbound Lakeshore Drive was flooded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large thunderstorm complex that developed over Nebraska and Iowa moved east southeast into Missouri and Illinois the evening of August 12th. It was diminishing as it approached the area, but still had enough power to cause wind damage into the early morning hours of August 13th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees were blown down as well as numerous large tree limbs.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large thunderstorm complex that developed over Nebraska and Iowa moved east southeast into Missouri and Illinois the evening of August 12th. It was diminishing as it approached the area, but still had enough power to cause wind damage into the early morning hours of August 13th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees were blown down along Highway 67.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large thunderstorm complex that developed over Nebraska and Iowa moved east southeast into Missouri and Illinois the evening of August 12th. It was diminishing as it approached the area, but still had enough power to cause wind damage into the early morning hours of August 13th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Co-op observer at the Alton Lock and Dam reported 60 mph winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large thunderstorm complex that developed over Nebraska and Iowa moved east southeast into Missouri and Illinois the evening of August 12th. It was diminishing as it approached the area, but still had enough power to cause wind damage into the early morning hours of August 13th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous tree limbs were blown down around the intersection of Illinois Route 13 and Warrensburg Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms fired along a cold front during the late morning and early afternoon hours of the 16th.  These storms produced several reports of high winds and wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous trees, large tree limbs, and power lines were blown down across Mattoon.|

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong to severe thunderstorms developed along and ahead of an outflow boundary.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous trees, tree limbs and power lines were blown down between Neoga, Toledo, and Greenup.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong to severe thunderstorms developed along and ahead of an outflow boundary.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A few trees, tree limbs, and power lines were blown down.  Some roofing material was torn off a building downtown.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain fell across parts of northern Illinois, including Rockford, causing significant flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Intersection of Route 47 and 120 closed due to two feet of standing water.  Willow and Clay Streets closed due to waist deep water.  Police conducting rescues from stranded vehicles. Basement flooding was reported throughout Woodstock, and as many as 10 properties were flooded as a creek overflowed its banks in McHenry, IL.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A thundestorm developed over northern Kane County during the afternoon of August 8th and quickly began to rotate and produced funnel cloud reports but no confirmed tornado touch downs.  Late in the afternoon, new thunderstorms produced flash flooding over parts of southern Kane County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Aurora Airport measured 3.22 inches in 84 minutes.  Significant flooding was reported at the intersections of Patricia and Snow Streets as well as Main Street and Highway 47.  Businesses and basements flooded in Big Rock, along with water over roads.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe storms moved across northern Illinois during the early morning hours of August 12th, producing wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 90 foot tall, 45 inch diameter tree was blown onto a house, causing significant damage to the roof, attic, a bathroom and a bedroom.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe storms moved across northwest Illinois during the early morning hours of August 14th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree and limbs were blown down near Lake Summerset and the Winnebago Stephenson County line.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms produced heavy rain across northern Illinois during the late morning and afternoon hours of August 19th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning struck in a parking lot, near a hospital causing a power surge which activated pumps in a laundry room, causing a bleach spill.  Several patients had to be relocated away from the fumes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large thunderstorm complex that developed over Nebraska and Iowa moved east southeast into Missouri and Illinois the evening of August 12th. It was diminishing as it approached the area, but still had enough power to cause wind damage into the early morning hours of August 13th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous large tree limbs were blown down throughout the city.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large tree limbs and power lines were blown down between Hardin and Batchtown.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: One foot diameter tree blown down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms developed during the early evening hours of July 18th and moved across northern Illinois.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Four to five inches of standing water Route 59 and Route 64.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms developed during the early evening hours of July 18th and moved across northern Illinois.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 10 inch diameter tree limb was blown down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms developed during the early evening hours of July 18th and moved across northern Illinois.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total rainfall of 4.03 inches measured in Plainfield near Route 126 and Route 30.  Storm total rainfall of 3.03 inches measured at the National Weather Service office.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms moved south across northeast Illinois during the early morning hours of July 26th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A microburst hit the Cedar Glen Subdivision in Homer Glen, east of Lockport.  This is just northwest of the intersection of 159th Street and Cedar Road.  There was scattered damage to trees throughout the neighborhood.  Objects such as trampolines and lawn furniture were blown over as well.  The most concentrated damage was in the east end of the subdivision along Cedar Glen Drive where several mature spruce trees were uprooted and some hardwood trees had large limbs or trunks snapped.  A large portion of a roof of a house was torn off.  Most of the damage was blown down from the northwest to southeast.  In the west end of the neighborhood, some of the limbs were blown more to the south and southwest, in a downburst pattern.  More than 30 trees were blown down at Old Oak Country Club.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain-producing showers and thunderstorms moved across eastern Iowa and northwest Illinois during the evening and overnight hours of July 18th.  An isolated storm downed some trees in Warren, IL around 7 PM.  Later, a bowing segment of thunderstorms pushed into Carroll County resulting in more wide-spread wind damage.  Rainfall totals ranged between three quarters of an inch to just under 3 inches, but due to saturated soils some flooding problems developed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds estimated to be 70 mph blew down trees and power lines.  Several trees fell onto some houses.  One person was transported to a hospital with a minor injury and later released.  Power and phone service was knocked out to about half of the town.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds estimated to be 70 mph blew down several trees onto Route 84 just south of Thomson.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds estimated to be 65 mph blew down several trees onto Chicago Ave.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large storm complex dove southeast across central and southeast Illinois during the morning hours of July 17th.  The complex produced very heavy rainfall, some flooding, and isolated reports of wind damage and large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A few tree limbs and power lines were blown down in Effingham.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong to severe storms moved across northern Illinois during the early morning hours of July 18th producing some wind damage and flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous large tree limbs blown down, some on houses.  Damage to sheds, signs and siding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front pushed into southeast Iowa and northwest and west central Illinois during the afternoon of July 10th sparking a line of showers and thunderstorms.  Two of the thunderstorms surpassed severe thresholds creating wind gusts between 65 and 75 mph.  One gust was recorded at the Quad City Airport in Moline, IL and the other gust downed some trees in Augusta, IL.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two large trees, 5 feet in diameter, were blown down.  Additional tree damage was also noted in the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High temperatures reached the mid 90s on July 8th and July 9th and near 90 on July 10th.  One person died from heat stress.
A cold front moved into northern Illinois during the afternoon hours producing severe thunderstorms which moved slowly and produced very heavy rain and flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning struck a house causing a fire in the attic, which sustained extensive damage.  The rest of the house was uninhabitable.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong to severe storms moved across northern Illinois during the early morning hours of July 18th producing some wind damage and flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Division and West Coven Streets flooded.  Polo measured 4.74 inches and 3 miles south of Oregon measured 5.19 inches.

Two of the thunderstorms surpassed severe thresholds creating wind gusts between 65 and 75 mph.  One gust was recorded at the Quad City Airport in Moline, IL and the other gust downed some trees in Augusta, IL.

A large storm complex dove southeast across central and southeast Illinois during the morning hours of July 17th.  The complex produced very heavy rainfall, some flooding, and isolated reports of wind damage and large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous trees and large tree limbs were blown down in parts of western Woodford county from thunderstorm winds.  In addition, an awning was damaged on a house in Roanoke.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm complex moved across portions of central Illinois to the north of a warm front.  This complex produced one report of damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Doors ripped off an outbuilding east of Urbana.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed ahead of a cold front.  Several of these storms were strong to severe in strength.  One storm produced wind damage in Shelbyville.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous trees, power lines and power poles were blown down on the south side of Shelbyville.

Rainfall totals ranged between three quarters of an inch to just under 3 inches, but due to saturated soils some flooding problems developed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds estimated to be 65 mph blew down a tree onto Route 84 between Thomson and Savanna.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of thunderstorms pivoted northeast into parts of southeast Iowa during the mid-morning hours of June 1st. Much of the area was just breaking out of a blanket of dense fog where visibilities dropped to less than a quarter mile. Dew point temperatures were in the middle 60s to around 70 degrees.||The line of storms appeared to become more broken through the late morning hours, while the area from Iowa City to Waterloo appeared to stratify out into a large area of showers. Just before 12:00 pm CDT, rapid intensification of storm cells on the southeast end of the original line occurred as it moved into northern portions of Louisa County.||A tornado touched down just south of Grandview, IA and moved northeast through Fruitland, IA and on to the southwest parts of Muscatine, IA. The tornado then lifted and as the storm cell continued to move northeast across Muscatine County. The super-cell re-intensified as it entered the southeast part of Cedar County just before 1 pm producing a brief tornado near Wilton, IA. The storm then moved across northwest parts of Scott County and Clinton County producing damaging wind gusts and large hail. The super-cell continued northeast into Jackson County producing a tornado near Bellevue around 2:30 pm, which moved across the Mississippi River into Jo Daviess County before lifting. The storm produced yet another tornado just south of Scales Mound, IL around 3:15 pm before moving into southwest Wisconsin and dissipating.||During the early afternoon hours, additional storms strengthened on the south end of the original line of storms, which went on to produce wind damage and large hail as they moved through northwest Illinois through the late afternoon hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail the size of a penny fell about 1 mile north of the Highland Spring Golf Course.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms moved east across northern Illinois during the late afternoon and early evening hours of June 1st producing mainly wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gust estimated to 60 mph on the Kane DeKalb County line.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved southeast across the Mid-Mississippi Valley during the morning. Short lines of thunderstorms with isolated damaging winds developed in advance of the front.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Extensive wind damage occurred in northwest and north central Saline County. A carport was blown on top of a mobile home. The wall of the mobile home was punctured by the carport. A large tree limb landed on the mobile home. Parts of a metal roof were peeled off another home. A large number of trees were down through the area. One tree landed on a pickup truck. Approximately 20 trees were uprooted or snapped in and around a cemetery. Some of the trees were greater than two feet in diameter. There was damage to cemetery buildings, including a chapel, gazebo, and outbuilding. Window panes were blown out of an adjacent church built in 1906, and cross beams were broken around the bell tower. A moving vehicle struck a downed tree at least 2 feet in diameter on Route 34. The driver was taken to a local hospital for a leg injury. The pattern and orientation of the damage suggested downburst winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved southeast across the Mid-Mississippi Valley during the morning. Short lines of thunderstorms with isolated damaging winds developed in advance of the front.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous trees were blown down from Crossville to Carmi. At Crossville, a home was heavily damaged when a large tree crashed through a kitchen window. Several other property owners in the Crossville area sustained minor wind damage. Trees were reported down over several roadways. A vehicle struck a downed tree in the Phillipstown area. Numerous power outages lingered through the day. Electrical service was restored to all customers within about 12 hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm reached severe levels during the heat of the afternoon. A weak upper level shortwave trough over the Plains, combined with a very moist south wind at the surface, was responsible for the activity.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind snapped about 20 trees along Old Route 13 and Illinois Route 13. Two of the trees briefly covered Old Illinois Route 13.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An isolated damaging wind event occurred in a thunderstorm associated with an upper level shortwave trough. The shortwave rotated southward around the back side of a stationary upper level low over the middle Atlantic states.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Extensive wind damage occurred, primarily on the west side of Carmi. Trees were snapped or uprooted, limbs were broken, and power poles were snapped. Some damage occurred to structures, such as the removal of the roof from a metal building and shifting of a rear garage wall. A travel trailer was rolled. A dumpster was blown onto a roadway. Debris from an equipment business was blown onto the parking lot of a nearby business. Some of the debris landed on vehicles. The damage area was approximately 1 mile by 1.5 miles. Very little or no damage was reported in downtown Carmi or eastern parts of town.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A persistent, weak 500 mb trough axis extending from northeast to southwest along the Ohio River Valley helped trigger more slow-moving thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Street flooding occurred due to heavy thunderstorm rains. Along one section of Illinois Route 13, water was up to the bumpers of cars. More than three-quarters of an inch of rain fell within an hour.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved southeast across the Mid-Mississippi Valley during the morning. Short lines of thunderstorms with isolated damaging winds developed in advance of the front.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large elm tree fell on part of a home's roof. The entire side of a nearby pole barn was blown away. Scraps of metal from the barn were blown across the street. Trees were downed in the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms moved east across northern Illinois during the late afternoon and early evening hours of June 1st producing mainly wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was blown down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms moved east across northern Illinois during the late afternoon and early evening hours of June 1st producing mainly wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees up to two feet in diameter were blown down.  A barn wall was blown in and windows were blown out of mobile homes.  Powerlines also blown down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms moved east across northern Illinois during the late afternoon and early evening hours of June 1st producing mainly wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees snapped in half or blown over.  Siding and shingles were ripped off of a house and heavy yard objects were tossed around.  A section of a plastic fence was broken and tossed.  Damage confined to a small area near the intersection of Shooting Park Road and Peoria Street.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A brief tornado touched down near Herscher.  No damage was reported.
A brief tornado touched down near Herscher.  No damage was reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A brief tornado touched down near Herscher.  No damage was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms moved east across northern Illinois during the late afternoon and early evening hours of June 1st producing mainly wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Vehicles damaged by blown over shelters, one minor injury.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms moved east across northern Illinois during the late afternoon and early evening hours of June 1st producing mainly wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A gazebo was blown over.  Estimated winds to 65 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong winds developed ahead of a cold front during the afternoon hours of June 7th.  The Chicago Streets and Sanitation Department reported 884 damaged trees, 71 traffic signals out, 45 light poles damaged and 76 downed power lines.  Trees and power lines were blown down in the surburbs as well.  Wind gusts were as high as 45 mph.
High temperatures climbed into the lower 90s during the middle of June.  Four people died from heat stress.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorms moved across parts of east central Iowa and northwest Illinois during the morning hours of June 21st.  The stronger storms produced pea to nickel size hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nickel size hail covered the ground.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: This was the fourth and most extreme of several freezes on consecutive nights. Extensive damage occurred to some crops, gardens, and even trees. Record warmth in March preceded these freezes. Most growth was well ahead of schedule when the record cold struck. Low temperatures were near records for the entire month of April. Many lows were in the lower 20's. Significant damage occurred to vegetation. In particular, the apple, peach, and grape crops were virtually wiped out. Unprotected strawberry plots were wiped out. The fledgling winter wheat crop was heavily damaged in addition to some early planted corn. Blossoms on dogwood trees and other flowering trees and shrubs were lost. Foliage on trees such as oaks, maples, and Bradford pears was shrivelled and discolored. Only a few trees that were already under stress were killed.

This was the third of several freezes on consecutive nights that resulted in extensive damage to some crops, gardens, and even trees. Record warmth in March preceded these freezes. Most growth was well ahead of schedule when the record cold struck. A large Canadian high pressure system drew unseasonably cold air into the region for several days. A hard freeze occurred during this third night of the freeze, when low temperatures were in the mid 20's. Significant damage occurred to vegetation.

A large and strong high in the upper levels of the atmosphere brought a resurgence of triple digit heat. Heat indices again topped 105 degrees on a couple of afternoons. This resurgence of heat was part of a longer term heat wave that lasted nearly all of August. At Carbondale, the preliminary August average temperature at the airport was 82.1 degrees. Using historical records from the Carbondale sewage plant, this ranked as the fourth warmest August on record. A streak of 21 consecutive days of highs of at least 90 degrees ended on August 19.

Severe drought conditions over western Kentucky at mid-month overspread nearly all of southern Illinois late in August. A persistent 500 mb high over the southeast and south central U.S. steered rain-producing systems north and west of the Lower Ohio Valley. Scattered thunderstorms provided what little rainfall there was. At Carbondale, the monthly rainfall was 1.55 inches. Soil moisture in the top 12 inches of soil was running 40 to 50 percent below normal. This impacted the agricultural sector. Statewide across Illinois, 45 percent of pasture land, 17 percent of the soybean crop, and 9 percent of the corn crop was rated poor or very poor. Due to high fire danger, an outdoor burn ban was imposed in Union County and the cities of Carbondale and Marion. A passing train sparked a fire on the railroad bridge over the Ohio River at Metropolis. About 50 railroad cross ties caught fire on the bridge.

A cluster of thunderstorms produced isolated wind damage as it tracked south to southwest through southern Illinois. The storms developed during the heat of the day along an inverted trough of low pressure extending northward from the Lower Mississippi Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A few motorists were stranded in floodwaters. A lot of localized flooding was reported.

Surface high pressure located over the Deep South remained nearly stationary. A persistent hot and humid southwest wind flow around this high brought an extended period of dangerously high heat indices, ranging from 105 to 110 degrees on several afternoons. A number of persons were treated for heat exhaustion, including 37 at a Carbondale hospital. Several counties opened a cooling shelter.

A persistent hot and humid southwest wind flow around this high brought an extended period of dangerously high heat indices, ranging from 105 to 110 degrees on several afternoons. A number of persons were treated for heat exhaustion, including 37 at a Carbondale hospital. Several counties opened a cooling shelter.

A number of persons were treated for heat exhaustion, including 37 at a Carbondale hospital. Several counties opened a cooling shelter.

Severe drought conditions over western Kentucky at mid-month overspread nearly all of southern Illinois late in August. A persistent 500 mb high over the southeast and south central U.S. steered rain-producing systems north and west of the Lower Ohio Valley. Scattered thunderstorms provided what little rainfall there was. At Carbondale, the monthly rainfall was 1.55 inches. Soil moisture in the top 12 inches of soil was running 40 to 50 percent below normal. This impacted the agricultural sector. Statewide across Illinois, 45 percent of pasture land, 17 percent of the soybean crop, and 9 percent of the corn crop was rated poor or very poor. Due to high fire danger, an outdoor burn ban was imposed in Union County and the cities of Carbondale and Marion. A passing train sparked a fire on the railroad bridge over the Ohio River at Metropolis. About 50 railroad cross ties caught fire on the bridge.
Isolated severe thunderstorms developed during the heat of the day. The storms were aided by a weak surface trough extending southeast from a weak low near the Iowa/Illinois border.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Roofs were damaged, and numerous trees were blown down by very strong thunderstorm winds. The wind damage was primarily from the east side of Fairfield eastward along Route 15. The entire roof was blown off a residence, and another residence was partially unroofed. The unroofed home sustained heavy water damage. Part of the roof of a church was torn off. A few very large pine trees were uprooted. At least a few of the downed trees landed on houses and vehicles. Three small telephone poles were blown down. A cornfield was flattened.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated severe thunderstorms developed during the heat of the day. The storms were aided by a weak surface trough extending southeast from a weak low near the Iowa/Illinois border.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous large tree limbs and a few large trees were blown down. Some of the tree limbs severed power lines. At least five utility poles were snapped.

A large and strong high in the upper levels of the atmosphere brought a resurgence of triple digit heat. Heat indices again topped 105 degrees on a couple of afternoons. This resurgence of heat was part of a longer term heat wave that lasted nearly all of August. At Carbondale, the preliminary August average temperature at the airport was 82.1 degrees. Using historical records from the Carbondale sewage plant, this ranked as the fourth warmest August on record. A streak of 21 consecutive days of highs of at least 90 degrees ended on August 19.

A cluster of thunderstorms produced isolated wind damage as it tracked south to southwest through southern Illinois. The storms developed during the heat of the day along an inverted trough of low pressure extending northward from the Lower Mississippi Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous trees were blown down from Harrisburg south to the county line. At the county fairgrounds in Harrisburg, the grandstands were heavily damaged, fencing was damaged, and utility poles were down. Trees landed on a cattle barn and concession stand. Elsewhere around the city, doors and windows of four businesses were blown out. A garage partially collapsed. Portions of the roof were lifted off an auto body shop and a utility service building. Large trees were uprooted and power lines were down in Harrisburg.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: This was the third of several freezes on consecutive nights that resulted in extensive damage to some crops, gardens, and even trees. Record warmth in March preceded these freezes. Most growth was well ahead of schedule when the record cold struck. A large Canadian high pressure system drew unseasonably cold air into the region for several days. A hard freeze occurred during this third night of the freeze, when low temperatures were in the mid 20's. Significant damage occurred to vegetation.

This was the fourth and most extreme of several freezes on consecutive nights. Extensive damage occurred to some crops, gardens, and even trees. Record warmth in March preceded these freezes. Most growth was well ahead of schedule when the record cold struck. Low temperatures were near records for the entire month of April. Many lows were in the lower 20's. Significant damage occurred to vegetation. In particular, the apple, peach, and grape crops were virtually wiped out. Unprotected strawberry plots were wiped out. The fledgling winter wheat crop was heavily damaged in addition to some early planted corn. Blossoms on dogwood trees and other flowering trees and shrubs were lost. Foliage on trees such as oaks, maples, and Bradford pears was shrivelled and discolored. Only a few trees that were already under stress were killed.

The fledgling winter wheat crop was heavily damaged in addition to some early planted corn. Blossoms on dogwood trees and other flowering trees and shrubs were lost. Foliage on trees such as oaks, maples, and Bradford pears was shrivelled and discolored. Only a few trees that were already under stress were killed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unseasonably intense low pressure system tracked from Wyoming to the Dakotas and then to northern Minnesota on June 7, 2007. A strong cold front moved across the region during the late afternoon and evening, which triggered scattered severe thunderstorms. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Many trees were blown down across the northern sections of Port Byron and tree limbs were downed in other parts of town by wind gusts estimated at 70 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of thunderstorms pivoted northeast into parts of southeast Iowa during the mid-morning hours of June 1st. Much of the area was just breaking out of a blanket of dense fog where visibilities dropped to less than a quarter mile. Dew point temperatures were in the middle 60s to around 70 degrees.||The line of storms appeared to become more broken through the late morning hours, while the area from Iowa City to Waterloo appeared to stratify out into a large area of showers. Just before 12:00 pm CDT, rapid intensification of storm cells on the southeast end of the original line occurred as it moved into northern portions of Louisa County.||A tornado touched down just south of Grandview, IA and moved northeast through Fruitland, IA and on to the southwest parts of Muscatine, IA. The tornado then lifted and as the storm cell continued to move northeast across Muscatine County. The super-cell re-intensified as it entered the southeast part of Cedar County just before 1 pm producing a brief tornado near Wilton, IA. The storm then moved across northwest parts of Scott County and Clinton County producing damaging wind gusts and large hail. The super-cell continued northeast into Jackson County producing a tornado near Bellevue around 2:30 pm, which moved across the Mississippi River into Jo Daviess County before lifting. The storm produced yet another tornado just south of Scales Mound, IL around 3:15 pm before moving into southwest Wisconsin and dissipating.||During the early afternoon hours, additional storms strengthened on the south end of the original line of storms, which went on to produce wind damage and large hail as they moved through northwest Illinois through the late afternoon hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado moved across the Mississippi River from just south of Lock and Dam 12 near Bellevue, IA and quickly lifted.  Some trees were damaged along the banks of the river.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Powerful and damaging thunderstorms moved across northern Illinois producing widespread wind damage.  The most intense wind damage occurred along a path which began in far western Dupage County near the intersection of Roosevelt Road and Washington Street in West Chicago and continued east through the northern portions Lombard and Glen Ellyn, into Northlake, then across the north side of Chicago to the Lake Michigan Shore near Montrose Harbor.  Wind damage along this path was consistent with winds 60 to 80 mph.  Smaller pockets of more intense damage with winds of 80 to 100 mph were noted in the Wrigleyville area.  The City of Chicago reported over 6,400 tree emergencies, over 200 damaged light poles, and over 350 downed power lines.  A 40 inch diameter tree was blown down on West Waveland Street in Chicago.  ComEd reported that a total of 615,000 customers lost power during the storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Significant flooding occurred along the Fox River throughout Kane County, In East Dundee, Richardson Subdivision was particularly hit hard by the flooding. In South Elgin, some of the hardest hit areas included South River Street, North Water Street, Arthur Avenue, Josephine Avenue, Ross Avenue, and Mavis Avenue. 10 Families were evacuated in Elgin as flood waters continued to rise on August 24th.  Bike paths and other green space along the river were inundated. Another area affected included the Valley View Subdivision in St Charles. Kane County officials estimated that flooding damaged more than 600 homes and businesses, and forced at least 80 residents from their homes. Flooding also occurred to 15 homes along the river���s west bank in Montgomery. Reports indicated that floodwaters affected backyards, garages, and basements of homes just south of the Ashland Avenue Bridge.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The first and only real Heat Wave of the summer began on August 4th and lasted through August 16th. High temperatures were consistently from the middle 90s to around 100 with the Heat Index from 105 to 110 degrees. Two deaths were reported in Madison County and one in St. Clair County. Two were found in homes that did not have air conditioning, and for the third the air conditioning was not working. Many schools across the area went to an early dismissal schedule in order to combat the heat.

The City of Chicago reported over 6,400 tree emergencies, over 200 damaged light poles, and over 350 downed power lines.  A 40 inch diameter tree was blown down on West Waveland Street in Chicago.  ComEd reported that a total of 615,000 customers lost power during the storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Measured 24 hour rainfall was 5.64 inches.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Following a week of heavy rainfall and a storm event rainfall of nearly five inches, significant flooding occurred in the northern two thirds of Grundy County on August 24th, as runoff worked it's way to ditches and streams that quickly overtopped their banks, followed by flooding along larger streams and the Illinois River.  Hundreds of basements filled with water, one rehabiliation center was evacuated in Morris, while a berm was built around the Morris hospital to protect the structure and prevent an evacuation and shutdown of that facility.  Local officials used more than 22,000 sandbags in an attempt to protect numerous structures from the rushing water.  In other locations, piles of gravel were used to divert the flow of water.  Many crops were damaged by flowing floodwaters or mud left on plants after the floodwaters receded.  An apartment complex was evacuated in Tudor Park due to flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The first and only real Heat Wave of the summer began on August 4th and lasted through August 16th. High temperatures were consistently from the middle 90s to around 100 with the Heat Index from 105 to 110 degrees. Two deaths were reported in Madison County and one in St. Clair County. Two were found in homes that did not have air conditioning, and for the third the air conditioning was not working. Many schools across the area went to an early dismissal schedule in order to combat the heat.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: After flash flooding from heavy rainfall on the evening of August 23rd, significant flooding continued across parts of central DeKalb County as rivers and streams overflowed their banks and flood waters took days to subside.  During the morning of August 24th, Route 30 and 1st Street were impassable.  A mobile home park was evacuated due to flood waters and Highway 64 was flooded with two feet of standing water. Flood waters inundated the Northern Illinois University campus and surrounding areas. Levees on campus were overtopped as water levels rose. Students from NIU helped sandbag in an attempt to hold back floodwaters. Roads and streets were closed due to flooding throughout DeKalb county and most schools were closed on the following Friday. Rescue workers evacuated residents from the Evergreen Village Mobile home Park on the east side of Sycamore as floodwaters inundated the entire area. Further downstream large areas flooded along the Kishwaukee River in the New Milford area. Campgrounds were inundated as well as streets and secondary roads. During the morning of August 24, Bethany Road was the only road crossing over the Kishwaukee River that was open to traffic. All other east-west road crossings had been closed. In total some 400 to 600 residents were displaced due to the flooding of the Kishwaukee River. The South Branch Kishwaukee River at DeKalb crested at 15.34 feet, the second highest crest on record. Storm total rainfall amounts included 4.85 inches in DeKalb, 3.50 inches in Waterman and 2.62 inches in Genoa.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The first of two rounds of severe weather occurred during the early morning hours. This first round was associated with strong low level southwest winds ahead of a deep low pressure center along the Missouri/Nebraska border. Most of the severe weather during the early morning hours was damaging wind associated with short lines of storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A number of downed trees and power lines were reported countywide, especially in West Frankfort and Christopher. A tree fell on top of a house in West Frankfort. Other damage in the county included destroyed gazebos, overturned carports, and destroyed chicken coops. Most of the downed power lines were due to trees falling on them.

The most intense wind damage occurred along a path which began in far western Dupage County near the intersection of Roosevelt Road and Washington Street in West Chicago and continued east through the northern portions Lombard and Glen Ellyn, into Northlake, then across the north side of Chicago to the Lake Michigan Shore near Montrose Harbor.  Wind damage along this path was consistent with winds 60 to 80 mph.  Smaller pockets of more intense damage with winds of 80 to 100 mph were noted in the Wrigleyville area.  The City of Chicago reported over 6,400 tree emergencies, over 200 damaged light poles, and over 350 downed power lines.  A 40 inch diameter tree was blown down on West Waveland Street in Chicago.  ComEd reported that a total of 615,000 customers lost power during the storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Measured gust to 70 mph.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several inches of flowing water over Route 23 in and north of Ottawa. Flash flooding occurred in Utica as Clark Run Creek overflowed its banks sending floodwaters down the main part of town. According to reports, about 45 residents responded to a request for evacuation. Widespread basement flooding was reported in the Peru-La Salle areas. Many streets and county roads flooded within La Salle County.  Flash flooding was also reported in Triumph, Naplate, and Grand Ridge in La Salle County. Dee Bennet Road near Buffalo Rock State park was closed due to flooding. Portions of Route 178 were also closed.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall over the upper part of the Des Plaines watershed in Wisconsin combined with well above normal precipitation over portions of Lake County Illinois brought the parts of Des Plaines River in flood by August 20th. The additional rains on August 23rd resulted in a crest of 8.62 feet the following evening at Des Plaines. In Des Plaines, flooding inundated the Methodist Campground. Many streets were affected by floodwaters and some homes on Big Bend Drive and Junior Terrace were surrounded by flood waters.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: This was the third of several freezes on consecutive nights that resulted in extensive damage to some crops, gardens, and even trees. Record warmth in March preceded these freezes. Most growth was well ahead of schedule when the record cold struck. A large Canadian high pressure system drew unseasonably cold air into the region for several days. A hard freeze occurred during this third night of the freeze, when low temperatures were in the mid 20's. Significant damage occurred to vegetation.

This was the fourth and most extreme of several freezes on consecutive nights. Extensive damage occurred to some crops, gardens, and even trees. Record warmth in March preceded these freezes. Most growth was well ahead of schedule when the record cold struck. Low temperatures were near records for the entire month of April. Many lows were in the lower 20's. Significant damage occurred to vegetation. In particular, the apple, peach, and grape crops were virtually wiped out. Unprotected strawberry plots were wiped out. The fledgling winter wheat crop was heavily damaged in addition to some early planted corn. Blossoms on dogwood trees and other flowering trees and shrubs were lost. Foliage on trees such as oaks, maples, and Bradford pears was shrivelled and discolored. Only a few trees that were already under stress were killed.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Torrential rainfall led to flash flooding in parts of DeKalb.  Numerous roads were closed with four feet of standing water.  Several people had to be rescued from cars in high water.  Numerous basements were flooded and a retail store on Sycamore Street was flooded.  A dorm on the Northern Illinois University Campus was evacuated.  Rainfall amounts ranged from 3 to 5 inches across DeKalb.  The highest amount was 5.00 inches at 13th and Scyamore Street, before the gage overflowed.

A persistent hot and humid southwest wind flow around this high brought an extended period of dangerously high heat indices, ranging from 105 to 110 degrees on several afternoons. The City of Chicago reported over 6,400 tree emergencies, over 200 damaged light poles, and over 350 downed power lines.  A 40 inch diameter tree was blown down on West Waveland Street in Chicago.  ComEd reported that a total of 615,000 customers lost power during the storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Cars were reported blown off a road north of Morris. A 62 mph gust was measured at Route 6 and Saratoga, two miles west of Morris.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The first and only real Heat Wave of the summer began on August 4th and lasted through August 16th. High temperatures were consistently from the middle 90s to around 100 with the Heat Index from 105 to 110 degrees. Two deaths were reported in Madison County and one in St. Clair County. Two were found in homes that did not have air conditioning, and for the third the air conditioning was not working. Many schools across the area went to an early dismissal schedule in order to combat the heat.

Heavy rain fell across parts of northern Illinois, including Rockford, causing significant flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Up to 4 inches of rain fell in a short time in the early morning hours. Some low lying roads had more than 2 feet of standing water. US41 near the Route 176 viaduct was closed due to flooding. One man had to be rescued from his vehicle. Route 176 was also closed at Sheridan Road due to flooding and buckling of pavement.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain fell across parts of northern Illinois, including Rockford, causing significant flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Over 6 inches of rain fell over southeast Rockford in a three hour period. The runoff quickly overwhelmed Keith and Madigan Creeks in Rockford as well as other streams across eastern Winnebago County. Hardest hit areas included the Keith Creek watershed in southeast Rockford, the same area that experienced flash flooding on Labor Day 2006. Twenty intersections flooded in Rockford with six cars stranded in flood waters.  Illinois Central railroad tracks washed out at Malford Road just south of Harrison.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall over the upper part of the Des Plaines watershed in Wisconsin combined with well above normal precipitation over portions of Lake County Illinois brought the parts of Des Plaines River into flood by August 20th. Additional rains on August 23rd resulted in crests during the following 3 days from points at Russell and Gurnee. Water inundated some homes along the river near Russell between the river and Kilbourne Road. Downstream at Gurnee, volunteers sandbagged around the Gurnee Grade School to prevent floodwaters from damaging the structure.  Flood waters inundated large areas of forest preserve along the river. Elsewhere in Gurnee flooding closed portions of Old Grand Avenue as well as Emerald Avenue and Kilbourne Road.  Sandbags were placed in front of several businesses along Old Grand Avenue. In North Libertyville Estates, a floodwall designed to hold flood waters from the Des Plaines River at bay, appeared to work as the area remained dry. In Libertyville, the Libertyville Golf Course at Riverside Park was flooded. The river crested at 10.55 feet at Russell on the 24th and at 9.82 feet on the evening of the 25th. High flows from near record flooding on the Fox River in Wisconsin were also moving downstream. These high flows, combined with excessive runoff over northern Illinois resulted in widespread flooding along the Fox River in the Chain of Lakes area. Homes in Antioch were surrounded by flood waters and many roads were closed. Most piers were under water and sandbagging efforts were ongoing.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain fell across parts of northern Illinois, including Rockford, causing significant flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding continued from heavy rains and flash flooding earlier on August 7th.  In Rockford, multiple roads closed due to flooding inlcuding Valencia, Avalon, and Churchill Grove from 9th to 20th. City officials in Rockford feared that water flowing over the spillway at the Alpine dam in Rockford would result in structural failure of the 65 year old dam. Residents in a 1200 home area immediately downstream of the dam along Keith Creek were evacuated as a precaution. Flood waters up to the first level of some houses and as many as 12 basement collapses were reported as a result of the flooding. Water came within 2 feet of flowing over the Alpine dam spillway at around 1130 AM. Severe flooding occurred within the Churchill Park area. Significant flooding also occurred along Madigan Creek in the Cherry Valley area. More than 40 residents along the creek were evacuated. About 30 homes along the creek sustained flood damage. Rainfall totals include 6.84 inches 3 miles southeast of Rockford, 6.79 inches northwest of Rockford and 4.70 inches 3 miles northeast of Rockford. The city of Rockford and Winnebago County were declared a state disaster area as a result of the flash flooding and flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system moved northeast across the Tennessee Valley, bringing a period of snow, sleet, and freezing rain. The highest accumulations of snow and sleet were along and north of Interstate 64. One to three inches fell in those areas. Mount Vernon reported about an inch of snow and sleet before a change to rain. Mount Carmel reported snow-covered roads due to 3 inches of snow and sleet. A narrow band of freezing rain produced up to a tenth of an inch of ice from about Benton eastward. Some vehicles spun out or slid into ditches along Interstate 57 from Benton northward to Mount Vernon. A period of sleet over the hills of Johnson, Pope, and Hardin Counties caused enough accumulation to slicken roads. Vehicles slid off Interstate 24 near Vienna.

A light mix of sleet and freezing rain caused slippery going for a short time. Just enough of the mixed precipitation fell to result in hazardous conditions on some surfaces. While main arteries were not affected, sidewalks and some secondary roads were icy. The affected area was mostly along and north of Interstate 64. The precipitation was caused by a southerly low-level wind flow of warmer air combined with a weak 500 mb trough over the Plains.

Severe thunderstorms developed during the afternoon hours of June 18th and moved across northerast Illinois, producing mainly wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 14 inch diameter tree limb was blown down and an empty pool caved in.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated severe thunderstorms developed during the heat of the day. A very weak 500 mb trough axis extended from west to east across the Lower Ohio Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter estimated a wind gust to 60 MPH.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Numerous thunderstorms preceded a strong cold front that moved southeast across the region. The storms organized into lines along outflow boundaries preceding the initial storms. Hail was the primary severe weather type.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large hail to the size of golf balls occurred west of Mount Vernon along Interstate 64. The golf-ball hail was reported at mile marker 70, about one mile east of the Woodlawn exit. Quarter-size hail fell in the Mount Vernon area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Additional thunderstorms moved across Stark and Marshall counties during the evening hours.  The damage associated with these storms was not as widespread as those earlier in the day.
The leading edge of a thunderstorm complex produced a strong outflow boundary with isolated reports of wind damage.
Severe storms moved across northern Illinois during the late evening hours of August 22nd.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Six to eight inches of standing water on Alexander just north of Route 53.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms moved across northern Illinois during the evening hours of Saturday, March 31st, producing winds in excess of 60 mph across many locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A gust to 70 mph was measured in Bristol.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms fired along and ahead of a cold front passing through central Illinois.  Several of the storms produced severe wind gusts.

Numerous thunderstorms preceded a strong cold front that moved southeast across the region. The storms organized into lines along outflow boundaries preceding the initial storms. Hail was the primary severe weather type.

Heavy rainfall on the 24th sent the lower Wabash and Big Muddy Rivers just above flood stage late in the month. The rainfall was caused by a large storm system that developed over the central Plains on the 24th and moved northeast toward the Great Lakes. Widespread amounts of 1 to 2 inches were common.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minor flooding occurred on the lower Wabash River. At Mount Carmel, the river rose above flood stage on the last day of the month. The flooding was minor. Bottomland fields were under water. The river continued to rise into early March.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front stalled across eastern Iowa and northwest Illinois during the morning hours of July 9th.  The front then acted as the focal point for shower and thunderstorm development through much of the day.  Torrential rains, hail up to the size of a quarter, and damaging winds were common across areas mainly over southeast and east central Iowa and northwest and west central Illinois.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A gravel and dirt road washed onto route 78 near Blue Mountain Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms moved east across northern Illinois during the late afternoon and early evening hours of June 1st producing mainly wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was blown down near Route 30 and Big Rock.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms moved across east central Illinois during the afternoon hours of May 15th, which produced hail and wind damage.
Strong thunderstorms moved across parts of northeast Illinois during the afternoon hours of May 26th, producing flooding across many areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Six inches of standing water reported at 159th Street and Cicero.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered showers and thunderstorms developed over the region ahead of an upper level system.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado touched down near the intersection of Woodlawn Park Drive and Lakeview Drive in the Crystal Lake Subdivision.  Numerous trees and tree limbs were blown down in the subdivision.  Also, one home sustained minor soffit and siding damage.  The tornado continued to the northeast, blowing down additional tree limbs and a couple of acres of corn on the south side of Sunderland Road as well as tree limbs on the north side of the road before lifting and dissipating.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms moved east across northern Illinois during the late afternoon and early evening hours of June 1st producing mainly wind damage.

A cold front moved through eastern Iowa, northeast Missouri, and northwest Illinois during the evening hours of September 30th producing showers and thunderstorms. Some of the thunderstorms were severe producing damaging winds of 60 to 70 mph and isolated nickel sized hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts estimated to be 60 mph blew down some tree limbs just east of East Moline, IL.  Time estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms developed during the early evening hours of July 18th and moved across northern Illinois.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Four to five inches of standing water at 167th and Cicero.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms developed during the early evening hours of July 18th and moved across northern Illinois.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A viaduct was flooded west of Cicero Avenue on 159th Street.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved through the region triggering severe thunderstorms.  Numerous reports of hail were received.
Lightning strikes took out virturlly all communications at the Police and Sheriff Department in Greenville, IL.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A lightning strike around 10:00 pm CDT took our virtually all communications at the Police and Sheriff Department in Greenville, IL. 911 service was out for several hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several rounds of showers and thunderstorms moved across eastern Iowa, northwest Illinois and northeast Missouri producing high winds and hail.  The area hardest hit was Kirkwood, IL where winds were estimated to be 100 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail the size of a nickel fell about 4 miles southwest of McNabb, IL.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated showers and thunderstorms developed across the region during the morning hours. One thunderstorm became severe causing some wind damage to one farmstead.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A microburst blew down a large oak tree onto the corner of a house, causing moderate damage.  Also, several smaller evergreens and fruit trees were blown down as well.  A nearby cornfield also sustained wind damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms with very heavy rain developed in a moist and unstable atmosphere ahead of a cold front over central Illinois.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Water was on a couple of streets in town.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved into northern Illinois during the afternoon hours producing severe thunderstorms which moved slowly and produced very heavy rain and flash flooding.
Thunderstorms produced heavy rain across northern Illinois during the late morning and afternoon hours of August 19th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Water flowing across Route 20 just east of Interstate 90.
